item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My upvote was sealed until that last addendum. As it is, I have to no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yuuup. My downvote will forever stand so long as that lolFoundationish and eyerollingly sexist addendum does.

(The canned vegetables remains amusing, but it's in no way enough.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others have said, the last addendum is utterly idiotic and ruins an otherwise good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not gonna lie, once I read "Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables." I laughed loud enough to earn a death glare from a coworker. I then reread the whole thing a la Lemony Snicket

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tend to like funny scips, but the last addendum just ruined it all. Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first attempt at following the instructions, provided the instructions are followed exactly, no matter how counter-intuitive they may sound (ex. addition of cat hair to meatball recipe; presence of tax accountant at child's birthday party), always results in a successful product (ex. incredibly flavorful meatballs; memorable and terror-free birthday party).

Experimentation logs show that after no less than 20 successful attempts at homemaking tasks, the instructions turn into detailed rituals, some of which mirror those which first were recorded in ███ ██████; some have never been previously documented. Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables.

Aaaaaaaaaand upvoted. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables" remains one of the funniest lines on this whole damn site. I love when my horror can make me giggle a little :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at Canned vegetables.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables.

…HOW IS IT NOT KETER?

But seriously though, this article was pretty interesting, and I do like the almost-too-silly aspect to it. What I want to know though, is what happens if an O5's wife (assuming they can have one) did the promotion ritual. They're already the highest up in the SCP Foundation, where would they go from there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the addendum sit right at the line between lolFoundation (and I did laugh) and creeping horror. So several staffs did it, and it work so well that the Foundation's top brass fail to see anything wrong with those promotion. They're most likely aware what's going on, yet can't do anything against it because the promotion is ensured.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It also note that male reader "will not notice anything counter-intuitive or out of the ordinary about instructions". So I think male reader will only find ordinary instruction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables."

Best "Arson, murder and Jaywalking" ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was having a good ol' time until the infant fat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea here, but the way this is described came off as really odd to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It means that if a male does it, no magic happens.

"…When book is read by a female, SCP-1954 enters active state and begins to show anomalous traits…"

This doesn't happen when a male reads it. In his hands, it's just printed text and pictures. When he follows the recipe, he just gets, as you say, meatballs with cat hair in them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"any male attempting such instructions will only be as successful as they would attempting such venture using any other source of instructions."

Ok, this part confuses me. Does this mean they'll experience the same success as the female, or would they just have moderately good meatballs with cat hair in them?

Also, not a huge fan of the addendum. I doubt the Foundation would ever allow SCPs to leave the site, or ever be used if they know they could be manipulated by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now there's a first order lolFoundation addendum. Shame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, the model this thing is working off is that of the stereotypically aspirational 50s housewife. That (again, stereotypical) model is highly focused on current standing and is at the very least uninterested in origins or the past, being preoccupied with the superficial aspects of traditional gender roles and current/future social and financial advancement.

That being the case, I'd expect the book to cue off a readers current state and the presence or lack thereof of the external characteristics that 50s suburban culture would associate with "femaleness" rather than their original configuration or the implications of their DNA.

In other words, if a reader regards herself as female (a non-negotiable prerequisite for gender-corrective surgery) and a superficial assessment by the (hypothetical) members of the 50s culture that gave rise to the books this SCP is modeled after would conclude that they were female (which there's every reason to think that it would), then for the books purposes, they are.

…I hope that makes sense. It was much clearer in my head >.<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is really good. I wonder how it would work on individuals, who had undergone sex-reassignment surgery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables."

I lol'd so hard at this. Despite that, there's JUST enough of a lack of jokes to keep this from being a -J to me. Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part with canned vegetables reminds me of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of Factory Porn for some reason. The basic idea of "cool thing that rapidly spirals into bad thing when used" remains the same, I guess?

incredibly flavorful meatballs; memorable and terror-free birthday party

I think it's generally assumed that most birthday parties are already terror-free. Or at least they have been in my experience.

A minor quibble: why is this in a reliquary? Unless there's a religious order of murderous housewives (I smell a new GoI), this doesn't seem particularly religiously or historically significant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good with the exceptions, as others have noted, of the mention of canned vegetables and the note at the end.

No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn. I really really want to upvote for the combined creep factor of normal housewive rendering kidnapped infants down into fat, and the overall quirkiness like tax accountants, cat hair, and (of course) canned vegetables.

But, I cannot in good conscience reward you for that terible lolFoundation note at the end. Now, keep in mind, I don't actually automatically downvote for lolFoundation, sometimes I find it genuinely amusing. But here? It's just bad.

I'm new here, so I'm not going to give you a pompous treatise on the "right way" to do lolFoundation, but I'll tell you that with this specific note, my biggest problem is the "researchers taking an SCP home" bit. In no way shape or form is that going to be tolerted at the Foundation. Which leads me to my next problem with this bit: If they can clearly link promotons to the effects of the SCP, I feel like the Foundation is more likely to punish the perpetrators rather then seemingly leave them with their shiny new ill-gotten positions.

So, overall, great work, but neutral vote. Not for the lolFoundation note itself, but for a poorly executed one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, very nice work. I upvoted for overall quality, but 2 small things bugged me.

1) Unless intentional for some reason I'm not catching, please remove the commas around the word "rituals" in this sentence:

Experimentation logs show that after no less than 20 successful attempts at homemaking tasks, the instructions turn into detailed, rituals, some…

2) I felt this "end-of-list surprise" was a bit too silly for a non-J article:

Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a shame, I liked the last part too, but there will be many a butt hurt over that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it all, especially those Satanic canned vegetables…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this overall.

However…

"No further experiments involving bringing SCP-1954 home to test on 'the wife' will be allowed. Department budget does not allow for any more promotions this fiscal year" Dr. ████████

…I hated that so much that I downvoted. It was like a kick in the balls after a mostly entertaining article, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three words for you: local abortion clinic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The note at the end after the last 'recipe' with the feathers and fat definitely added a major squick factor. Picturing that being done, and all… Ugh. But by the way, do raven feathers come in a color other than black?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No no no, only the *one* got an ingredient list including baby fat.
Although the neighborhood of Doctor Blackbox had a strange rash of petunias being deflowered.
And Professor Redacted never did find his spare spectacles.
Oh, and we don't talk about what happened to Junior Researcher Corbette's uncle… Shame he was demoted as an example of the last one to use the book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No true homemaker would give up an opportunity to make an ingredient "from scratch"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. My only problem is that you seem to have a serious aversion to articles like "the" and "a". If that's a stylistic choice, it's not one I care for. I mentally added them throughout, like so: "No marks appear elsewhere on the cover, including the spine. A [d]ust jacket, if ever present, is missing."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Page, noun.
1.
a. A leaf or one side of a leaf, as of a book, letter, newspaper, or manuscript: tore a page from the book.
b. The writing or printing on one side of a leaf.
c. The type set for printing one side of a leaf.

Source.

Page, noun.
1. one side of a leaf of something printed or written, as a book, manuscript, or letter.
2. the entire leaf of such a printed or written thing: He tore out one of the pages.

Source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And thus is the differential between last page and last numbered page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What with all the promotions in that department, no one has asked the most basic of questions…

Where in the Hell is everyone getting "16 ounces rendered fat from infant (human)"?!

I mean, seriously!? What, is "Big Lots" having a sale, or something?!

Everyone in that department, report to Human Resources for immediate demotion to D-Class and subsequent re-assignment…! (You bunch of sickos!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For illustration, the last page of the paperback that happens to be sitting on my desk is "387". It is the physically last page in the book. You have to turn over 8 or 9 sheets of paper, depending on if you count the cover, before you get to "1"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it had an odd number of sheets, it would still have twice as many pages. Front and back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both you and wishun make good defense of leaving the additional note in. The humor doesn't bother me as much as it did when I first commented (since the rest of the article is funny-creepy already, and it's a good mix).

But, I can't get behind anyone taking an SCP home with them. Your original one, about the book being used around the office to curry favor, is a better fit. It keeps the funny sting at the end of the article, while also implying that foundation staff are doing the ol' baby rendering thing to get a promotion. Maybe make it a little less slap-on-the-handy on the part of the author of the note, have them borderline freaked out about the book being used this way,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I think I would like it better if each lady got her own instructions that only work for her, but they will always work for her. It removes some of the "ummm….magic?" that you are worried about, but preserves the weirdness. It changes the book from a reality warping talisman into a record of loopholes in the laws of reality.

As to the end-note, I encourage you to keep it. You're rating is strongly positive at the moment, so the majority think that it at least doesn't kill the piece. It is fashionable to auto-hate certain things recently, see the "Standard Form for Downvotes" that went up the other day. Lolfoundation is the one I disagree with the most. Good lolfoundation that is well-executed and flows naturally from the circumstances, like what you've got here, should be preserved. If nothing else, having good examples to learn from may cut down on the volume of bad examples people attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole witches-of-Stepford angle is not bad, oddly. That end note really needs to go though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables.

And then I upvoted.

This is both quirky and dark, though I think the final note might be a little too lolfoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: they're potential housewives so yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bear with me, I'm addressing a bunch of everyone's feedback here.

Wishun, good question, in a very early first draft (which I didn't post here at all) that was addressed, if the instructions were not followed directly from the book (as in you wrote down the recipe and tried it again later), you'd get awful results like one would expect from meatballs filled with hair. However, I…uh…expunged a lot of that kind of stuff in an early re-write because it smacked too much of being all because of MAGIC, which I know is not a good thing for an SCP. I also had a blurb in it about the fact that it had 333 pages (I knew someone would get technical about that) but again, I felt like that went too much into "because…magic!" territory, and I couldn't really figure out a way to explain it, so I scrapped it, but I like the thought that I'm just saying there are 333 NUMBERED pages, as its nicely convenient and still has that whole half-satanic aspect.

If I can think of a good way to reincorporate that into the story, I'll do so. I've got a lot of things to think about based on feedback. I know not everyone who's read this agrees with this, but I feel like its JUST on this side of being a J while still retaining some creep factor. I am worried that if I do too many edits I'm going to lose a lot of that one way or the other, and my goal wasn't to write the WORLD'S DARN BEST SCP EVERRRRRR, but to write a memorable oddity. I changed a lot of it around when I first posted yesterday, and now I'm afraid to change much more…

At any rate, I'm really happy with all the feedback. I know not many people are fond of the final note, but I feel like that jarring shift is kind of a trademark for me in writing style (this isn't the first thing I've ever written, but certainly my first crack at this style of writing), so while I am tempted to lose it to appease everyone, but I like it.

So I'll continue to mull over this feedback and see what I can take to improve the article without losing my personal touch. In the meantime everyone continue to suggest things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The foreword is apparently on page 1, implying that the title page and contents and dedication and stuff before it might be numbered i, ii, iii, iv etc., so even though the final page could say “333” on it, maybe there are in fact slightly more actual pages than that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like (good) lolfoundation, I like old school, and I like this.

One questions that bug me: Can the instructions be followed if not read from the book? What I mean is, if Debbie the D-class makes Meatballs a la cat hair with the book open in front of her, of course they turn out durn tasty. But if Debbie has a very good memory and is able to repeat the steps exactly, sans "Helen's Hints", are the results equally tasty or do they turn out as the hair-balls you would normally expect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea itself is arresting (that's a compliment), but the execution and its tone whiplash leave something to be desired. It's wholly possible to mix humor and horror with good effect, but this instance is too heavy-handed. That "murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables" didn't quite work for me this time around, but I honestly think it could be highly effective given a more subtle overall treatment.

No vote from me. You can do better — you're so close!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either it's anomalous, or it has an odd number of sheets of paper, which is perfectly plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. Also, one other tiny, tiny picky thing, as a person who works in book publishing and knows how these things work:

the title Helen Homemaker's Hints For The Harried Housewife stamped on front with flaking gold ink.

Gold ink exists, but I don't think that's what you mean. Foil embossing or foil stamping is what you're thinking of for a case-bound book that would have a dust jacket.

/pedantry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dare I ask how the book has an odd number of pages?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon subsequent uses of book, instructions are reported to become more out of the ordinary (ex. hints for cleaning silver includes rubbing objects with blood;

Aaaaaaaand I start holding my breath hoping this isn't going to turn into corn.

Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables.

Forgiven.

Ensuring He Gets That Promotion At Work

Ingredients:

16 ounces rendered fat from infant (human)

24 black raven feathers

Aaaand you lost me. I liked the concept, I still do, I just think it could have been taken in a more original direction. +1 Regardless, despite some annoyances here and there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "canned vegetables" thing really worked for me, not only because it's funny as hell, but because it's so surreal.

I think the additional note needs to go - I'm not really fond of lolfoundation stuff, and I think it goes without saying that you can't take an SCP home with you, EVER, even if you are really in the mood for tasty cathair meatballs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good idea in my eyes, and the execution segues nicely from "strange" to "horrifying"… right up until that bit in the end with promotions. Going from "smearing rendered infant fat on your [DATA EXPUNGED] and getting your [DATA EXPUNGED] on with your spouse" to someone going "Haha, please no more of THAT please, the budget can't stand it!" is incredible jarring and spoils the effect.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "Most of these rituals involve murder, cannibalization, sexual encounters, self mutilation, and canned vegetables." +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, it works for me by virtue of being black humour. Well, except for the note at the end. It's like a clown at someone's funeral.

It's also true that some of the highest rated articles on the site attempt humour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This is the sentence where it becomes one hundred percent clear that the author isn't taking their own idea seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Description has very few “a” or “the”. Gives document sense of trying too hard to be formal. I give this downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was, actually, talking about the sudden "canned vegetables". I mean you have four pretty disturbing things and then bam, vegetables? It is quite random and if made for lulz, it makes the SCP both funny and serious, and I'm not sure if it can work out both ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Area to be monitored at all times by armed guard

By "an armed guard", or more than one armed guard?

It is noted that any male observers reading instructions presented in book will not notice anything counter-intuitive or out of the ordinary about instructions,

How do they know that the odd instructions are there but not noticed, as opposed to the book having different (and completely ordinary) content when viewed by a man?

Upon subsequent uses of book, instructions are reported to become more out of the ordinary (ex. hints for cleaning silver includes rubbing objects with blood; instructions for throwing cocktail party involves putting arsenic in canapes). Even with such alterations to commonly accepted homemaking functions, results are always successful

Does this mean that the canapes are non-poisonous in spite of the presence of arsenic?

During experiments, subjects have been persuaded to transcribe instructions exactly as they see them in book.

So if the pages are photographed by someone else while a subject is reading it, do the photos show something different than what the subject is perceiving?
